So look at the one on page 87. I'll help you even though it's tiny print. You don't have to read very far. Tell me where the, let them off the hook. It's not your fault that you haven't been rich up until now. Tell me where it is. See if you can find it. I'll give you a minute. Anybody yet? Very good. Very good. It's in the headline. That's exactly correct. It's right there in the headline. The headline says, too busy earning a living to make any money. The guy says, well yeah, that's exactly the friggin problem. That's why I'm not rich. I'm too damn busy earning a living. So we let them off the hook in the headline. It's brilliant. Now I don't know if Joe knew what he was doing. I have no idea. But it's right there at the very beginning. Very cool. The next one I gave you on 91, starting on 91, is two versions. So there's one version on 92. There's one version on 93. The one on 93 is easier to read. This is an ad I wrote. And it ran all sorts of places. It was rather daring at the time, because most of the Get Rich Ads were selling 10-buck books. And this one-step sale is a $300 course. Which I guess in today's dollars, nearly a grand probably. So this is a one-step sale of what today would be about a $1,000 item from an ad. And in magazines where there were $10 book ads, all around it. So it was rather daring at the time. The emphasis of the ad is really on just a couple of big ideas. Something important, the first paragraph presents facts. They probably, it's questionable if they were real facts, but it presents facts nonetheless, which are important for the rest of the premise to be accepted. The last column, so if you're looking at 93, which is the easier one to read, the last column has a big winner versus loser pick which you are element to it. This ad was front-end positive everywhere it ran but one. So it's kind of an interesting study. And it again does not actually disclose any real details. Page 94 gives you, and then the pages behind it give you a couple examples. This is a grand old master of mystery opportunity ads. So Bud ran ads like this for years. And like this one, they usually had, they usually, not always, but they usually had a photograph in them of somebody doing something rather incomprehensible. Wearing a goofy hat, their head buried in a bucket, something. And violated the headline rules because a great number of the ads were very much like this. Can you tell what this woman is doing, what the hell is this woman doing? They sold one ad, sold 1.6 million copies of a book, which I don't care about any standards, is a crap load of books, right? There's, again, there's no promise up top, so that's kind of interesting. But they're very disarming stories. And we're going to talk about disarming copy in a little bit. And they have a, if you look at page 96, and I'm going to show you a different version of this later, but you'll see a box in the middle called Try This Test. To the best of my knowledge, Bud was the first one to do this in opportunity advertising, give somebody some kind of way they could prove the validity of what was being said to them. And some are more interesting than others. This one's pretty weak, actually, but it almost doesn't matter, because the idea is planted that you proved to yourself that this works. So on our list now, one of the elements of an opportunity pitch that we can use to get them to act contrary to what their conscious mind is telling them is hype. The next one, number two, is trust. They are not mutually exclusive, although arguably they should be. In a few minutes, we're going to run the infomercial. Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah. So could you cue that up? So opportunity marketers, so what we can learn from them, is they are often having to establish trust for essentially very untrustworthy propositions. So they are telling people that with two type pages, money will fall out of the sky. Doing this weird little thing with a jar is somehow going to give you thousands of dollars a month. They are not stepping forward with anything has, has giggle proof, has most of the propositions you are stepping forward with. And yet, if they can't establish trust, they pretty much can't make a sale. So what they do, what they have to do to establish trust will work even better for you. And so it ought to be paid close attention to. There's basically four things they all do on page 98. So it's basically four things they all do. So one is they present a person who they have a big story for. It can be themselves, it can be somebody else.
